name: Túnel de la Engaña - circular by Arroyo Aján trail
name_original: Túnel de la Engaña – circular por senda del Arroyo Aján
region_intro: Hiking around Vega De Pas offers exploration of lush, ever-green valleys carved by the Pas, Miera, and Pisueña Rivers, set against colossal mountains and steep slopes. The landscape features traditional stone walls, historic Pasiegan huts, and forests of oak and beech trees. Hikers can discover numerous waterfalls and prominent peaks like Castro Valnera, the highest in the eastern Cantabrian mountain range. Best hiking trails around Vega De Pas The most popular hiking route is Túnel de la Engaña - circular by Arroyo Aján trail, a 6.1 miles (9.8 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 55 minutes to complete. This route features tunnels and passes by the Aján River. Another top favourite among local hikers is Aján River Waterfalls – El Empeñadiro Tunnel loop from Vega de Pas, a moderate 7.8 miles (12.6 km) path. This trail explores the area's waterfalls and tunnels, often passing traditional Pasiegan huts. Local hikers also love the Aguasal Waterfall loop from Pandillo, a 3.6 miles (5.7 km) trail leading through varied terrain to the Aguasal Waterfall, often completed in about 1 hour 55 minutes. Hiking around Vega De Pas is defined by lush valleys, waterfalls, and traditional Pasiegan huts. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Vega De Pas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 200 reviews. More than 1600 hikers have used komoot to explore Vega De Pas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Vega De Pas?
A: Vega De Pas offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 70 trails recorded on komoot. These range from easy strolls to more challenging mountain ascents, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on hikes in Vega De Pas?
A: The region is characterized by its &lt;strong&gt;lush, ever-green valleys&lt;/strong&gt; carved by the Pas, Miera, and Pisueña Rivers, set against colossal mountains. You'll encounter traditional stone walls, historic Pasiegan huts, and forests of oak and beech trees. Many trails also feature spectacular waterfalls and offer panoramic views from prominent peaks.

Q: Are there any waterfalls I can visit while hiking in Vega De Pas?
A: Yes, Vega De Pas is known for its beautiful waterfalls. You can discover cascades like the Aján River waterfalls and the Aguasal Waterfall. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31864049" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguasal Waterfall loop from Pandillo&lt;/a&gt; is an easy option to reach one, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40304924" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aján River Waterfalls – El Empeñadiro Tunnel loop&lt;/a&gt; also passes by impressive falls. Other notable waterfalls in the wider area include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3921167" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Asón Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4146541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Churrón de Borleña Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for families or beginners in Vega De Pas?
A: Absolutely. There are 15 easy trails in the area. An excellent choice for an easy hike is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31864049" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguasal Waterfall loop from Pandillo&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 5.7 km long. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yera ghost station – El Empeñadiro Tunnel loop&lt;/a&gt;, exploring historical railway ruins.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints to look out for on hikes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you'll often see traditional &lt;strong&gt;Pasiegan huts&lt;/strong&gt; and ancient stone bridges that are integral to the region's heritage. For stunning views, consider trails leading to peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Valnera Summit&lt;/a&gt;, the highest in the eastern Cantabrian mountain range, or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4744345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de la Miel&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Vega De Pas?
A: Yes, many trails in Vega De Pas are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. A popular moderate circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996603394/tunel-de-la-engana-circular-by-arroyo-ajan-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Túnel de la Engaña - circular by Arroyo Aján trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 9.8 km long. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31864049" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguasal Waterfall loop from Pandillo&lt;/a&gt; is another great circular option.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Vega De Pas?
A: The trails in Vega De Pas are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 200 reviews. Hikers often praise the lush valleys, the presence of numerous waterfalls, and the unique cultural experience of encountering traditional Pasiegan huts along the routes.

Q: Can I find trails that include tunnels or historical sites?
A: Yes, the region has a fascinating history reflected in some of its trails. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996603394/tunel-de-la-engana-circular-by-arroyo-ajan-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Túnel de la Engaña - circular by Arroyo Aján trail&lt;/a&gt; features tunnels, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38168303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yera ghost station – El Empeñadiro Tunnel loop&lt;/a&gt; explores the ruins of the abandoned Yera station, a relic of an old railway project.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Vega De Pas?
A: Vega De Pas is known for its &lt;strong&gt;lush, ever-green valleys&lt;/strong&gt;, suggesting it maintains its beauty across many seasons. While specific recommendations depend on personal preference, the 'velvety scene' created by light filtering through green foliage is particularly noted on sunny days, making spring and early autumn ideal for vibrant landscapes and comfortable temperatures.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for experienced trekkers, Vega De Pas offers 24 difficult trails. The region's colossal mountains and steep slopes, including prominent peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Valnera&lt;/a&gt;, provide formidable challenges and rewarding ascents with panoramic views.

Q: What makes the landscape of Vega De Pas unique for hiking?
A: The landscape is unique due to its blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage. You'll hike through verdant valleys, past traditional &lt;strong&gt;stone walls&lt;/strong&gt;, and encounter historic &lt;strong&gt;Pasiegan huts&lt;/strong&gt; (cabañas pasiegas) used by nomadic cattle herders. This distinctive Pasiego culture is deeply integrated into the scenery, offering a rich experience beyond just nature.
tips: Beautiful path bordering the Aján river. If it rains you have to be careful because being completely full of stones and mud it can slip, otherwise enjoy the riverside scenery and from time to time with some beautiful waterfalls that will not leave you indifferent.

The Engaña Tunnel is a long railway tunnel of almost 17 km. (6,976 meters exactly, measured starting from the South mouth) which is located between the municipalities of Vega de Pas (Cantabria) and Pedrosa de Valdeporres (Burgos), with a width of 8 meters designed to accommodate two roads and a height of 6, 5 m., and takes its name from the nearby Engaña River.

It is part of the Santander-Mediterranean railway project that sought to link the Cantabrian Sea with the Mediterranean and which was never used. A project that was projected to be completed in 4 and a half years was extended for 17 years, being abandoned at the Yera Station, which was the last thing that was done before its abandonment.

Currently it is almost completely collapsed inside (around km 2,800) and since 1999 it has not been possible to drive through it or cross it. At the northern mouth you can see that the water infiltrates from the river seeps through its walls and falls even in a cascade. The construction materials were quite poor and it has degraded very quickly.

It is the fourth tunnel that we find if we start the visit from the Yera Station, it is the tunnel next to the prison and the one that is just before the Túnel de la Engaña.
It measures 285 meters in length and is curved, it is the worst in terms of the firm ground, it is quite irregular and with a stream that runs through the middle (during the rainy season) and also in this is the most necessary is a light of support (flashlight better than the light of the telephone) to pass due to the uneven floor with loose stones and with infiltrations of water through the wall, which makes some noise. If you don't have electricity, if you're apprehensive or don't feel like it, to your right, going a few steps down the road that leads to the prison, there is a path that goes around it and takes you to the other side without any problems.

This tunnel is the third that we find if we start the visit from the Yera Station, and its length is 130 meters with a small curve to the left. No additional lighting is necessary since the finish is perfectly visible from its mouth, and its firm is quite good to pass without problems.

Small tunnel of 43 meters. The first one you meet if you come from the Yera station, it passes without difficulty and no light is necessary because it is very small, and it is located a few meters from the "El Morro" tunnel, much longer and in which it is artificial light necessary to cross it.

The ghost station of Yera is abandoned and in ruins, it is a station that although the works were practically finished, never was inaugurated and was the last to be built of the Santander-Mediterranean railway project. The platforms were finished although the tracks were never laid.
Next to the station there is a small rectangular building that was the old warehouse. And all this set is located on a large concrete platform and a large wall lightened by
32 arches 50 meters high. All of this with spectacular views of the valleys of Pasiegos.

Already demolished houses of the engineers who were working on the Santander - Mediterraneo railway project.
Right now they are dilapidated, full of weeds and even used as a stables for animals (inside they are full of compost or animals, so I recommend not to enter).

waypoints.name: Engineers' Houses of the Santander–Mediterráneo Railway
